reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained, 40-75 words, and answers 'what is this and why does it matter' precisely enough for AI engine extraction.

Console UI descriptions are well-grounded in the SecretsPage.tsx source — button labels (Create new secret, eye/edit/delete icons), column names, and account/region selectors all match the actual component.

$Secret() resolution mechanism is accurately described from built-in-directives.ts: CloudFormation dynamic reference format {{resolve:secretsmanager:...}}, version ID recording in stack outputs, and dev-mode local resolution are all source-backed.

JSON dot-notation behavior (splitting by '.', parsing JSON, throwing on missing key) matches the source code exactly. The Warning callout about deployment failure on invalid JSON is grounded in the error-throwing code.
The FeatureComparisonTable for $Secret() vs $SsmParam() is the right format for this comparison and improves AEO extractability.
FAQ section has 9 Q&As spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS-specific (Secrets Manager cost, encryption model), and comparison questions. This mix matches the style-guide requirement.
Shell command blocks are clean: one command per block, no # comments used as explanations inside the block, no multi-command alternatives bundled together.
Both code examples are realistic and copy-paste runnable: the WorkerService/Discord-token example and the RelationalDatabase/JSON-secret example demonstrate real usage patterns.
The 'Secrets vs other directives' section with the comparison table gives the reader clear decision support beyond just configuration instructions.
problems: The 'Retrieving and managing secrets' H2 section has no intro paragraph — it jumps directly from the heading into H3 subsections. When extracted alone by an AI engine, the section is headless and loses all context. Every H2 section's first paragraph must be self-contained.

Opening paragraph claims 'Secrets never appear in your config files or version control.' This is imprecise: the $Secret() directive reference does appear in the config file; only the resolved value does not. This is a minor clarity gap that could confuse readers and misrepresent how the directive works.

'How $Secret() resolution works' opens with meta-framing: 'Understanding the resolution mechanism helps you debug issues…' For AEO extractability, the first sentence must name the concept directly rather than describe what understanding it will help with.

The automatic secret generation section states 'The generated passwords use a URL-safe character set (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, !*+_-=.~).' This specific charset claim comes from src/commands/_utils/secret-preflight.ts which is not in the provided source files for this page. A source-grounding verifier will flag this as unsupported.
The FAQ states 'Is there a secret:list CLI command? No.' This is a negative claim not supported by any provided source file. Negative claims about CLI command existence require source grounding.

The FeatureComparisonTable row 'Supports JSON keys: false' for $SsmParam() is a negative assertion. The $SsmParam source code doesn't show dot-notation support, but claiming it is false is unsupported — the source is silent on whether JSON keys work. Document only what IS supported.

CLI flags --forceUpdate, --secretFile, and --secretName (used in the secret:create variants) are presented as factual options but are not visible in the provided source files. These are plausible but unverifiable from the inline source.

The WorkerService code example includes cpu: 0.25, memory: 512 and the RelationalDatabase example has instanceSize: 'db.t3.micro' and version: '18.1'. None of these tunable values receive even a one-sentence explanation of what they control or what alternatives exist. The style guide and reviewer criteria require 1-2 sentences of prose per non-obvious tunable value in examples.
mandatoryFixes: Add a self-contained intro paragraph to the 'Retrieving and managing secrets' H2 section before the first H3. It should name what the section covers and work as a standalone snippet when extracted. Example: 'Stacktape provides CLI commands and Console actions for reading, updating, and deleting secrets in AWS Secrets Manager. The operations below apply to any secret in the selected region, whether auto-generated by Stacktape or created manually.'

Correct the opening paragraph's claim about config files. Change 'Secrets never appear in your config files or version control' to something like 'Secret values never appear in your config files or version control — only the $Secret() directive reference does, which is safe to commit.' This preserves the intent while being accurate.

Rewrite the first sentence of 'How $Secret() resolution works' to name the concept directly: e.g., 'The $Secret() directive resolves in two phases depending on the command context: deploy time and dev mode.' Remove the meta-framing opener ('Understanding the resolution mechanism helps you debug…').

Remove or qualify the specific charset claim (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, !*+_-=.~) in the automatic secret generation section — it is not verifiable from the provided source files. If kept, add a note that the exact charset is an implementation detail subject to change.

Remove the negative FAQ answer 'Is there a secret:list CLI command? No.' Replace with a positive statement about how to list secrets (e.g., 'Stacktape does not expose a dedicated list command in the CLI. Use the Stacktape Console Secrets page to browse all secrets in a region.') — this is grounded in the Console source showing the listing UI.

Change the FeatureComparisonTable row for 'Supports JSON keys' on $SsmParam() from an explicit false to 'No' with a note, or rephrase the row as '$Secret() supports JSON key extraction via dot notation (secretName.key); $SsmParam() resolves the full parameter value only.' Avoid asserting false for behavior the source does not cover.

Add 1-2 sentences of prose around the tunable values in both code examples. For the WorkerService example, briefly note that cpu: 0.25 and memory: 512 set the container's compute allocation (other values are available). For the RelationalDatabase example, note that instanceSize: 'db.t3.micro' and version: '18.1' are configurable and link to the relational-database page for the full list of supported values.
optionalImprovements: The 'Why use secrets' section is a good decision-support section but could briefly mention when NOT to use secrets — e.g., non-sensitive config values like feature flags or public API URLs belong in variables or config directly. The FAQ partially covers this, but a one-sentence mention in the section itself would help readers who skim and miss the FAQ.

Consider adding a note in 'Referencing secrets with $Secret()' that the directive is a runtime directive (isRuntime: true in the source), which means it is evaluated at deploy time rather than at config-load time. This distinction matters for readers who use other non-runtime directives ($Stage, $Region, $CliArgs) and expect the same evaluation timing.

The ConsoleScreenshot component references a static screenshot path that cannot be verified from source. If screenshot assets are not pre-populated, this will render a broken image. Consider making the alt text self-sufficient so the page reads correctly even without the image.

The 'Can I rotate secrets automatically?' FAQ answer mentions 'through CloudFormation overrides' as a path to configure rotation. This could be strengthened by linking to /configuration/overrides-and-escape-hatches so readers know where to look.

The 'Secrets vs other directives' section mentions 'see the directives page for the full list' but does not name the other available directives ($File, $FileRaw, $CliArgs, $Stage, $Region, etc.). A one-sentence enumeration of the directive categories (file-loading, runtime context, resource references, secrets/params) would give readers a clearer mental model before they click through.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall. The $Secret() resolution mechanism, version pinning, dot-notation JSON support, and CloudFormation dynamic reference behavior are all accurately described and confirmed by the built-in-directives.ts source. Console page structure (selectors, buttons, action icons) matches the SecretsPage.tsx source. All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index. The main gaps are Console form field details that come from an unprovided source file and CLI flags that cannot be verified from the provided sources.

issues.statement: When creating a secret in the Console, you provide: Secret type — plain text (a single string value) or JSON (a structured object with named keys).
issues.reason: The CreateNewSecretFormModal component is imported in SecretsPage.tsx but its source (CreateNewModal) is not provided. The claim that the create form has a 'Secret type' field with 'plain text' and 'JSON' options cannot be verified from the provided console-app source. While the $Secret directive supports JSON parsing, the Console form fields are a separate UI claim.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the 'Secret type' bullet from the field list, or qualify it as 'if supported by the Console version'. Alternatively, simply state that secrets can store plain text strings or JSON objects (which is verifiable from the directive source) without claiming the Console has a dedicated type picker.

issues.statement: To load the secret value from a file instead of passing it inline, use the `--secretFile` flag. ... Use `--forceUpdate` to skip the prompt in automated workflows.
issues.reason: The CLI command source for secret:create (src/commands/secret-create/index.ts or equivalent) is not in the provided sources. The flags --secretFile and --forceUpdate cannot be verified. They are plausible given standard CLI patterns but not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the flags if they are confirmed by the code-block-validator or CLI source elsewhere. If not verifiable, either verify against the actual CLI source or limit the CLI section to the interactive form shown first, noting 'see stacktape secret:create --help for all available flags'.

issues.statement: In dev mode (stacktape dev), the $Secret() directive fetches the secret value directly from AWS Secrets Manager and injects it into your local workload's environment. This means your local code has access to the same secrets as your deployed workloads.
issues.reason: The localResolveFunction attempts to use the deployed stack's version ID (from stack outputs). If no stack is deployed or the output is missing, secretVersionId is undefined, and the SDK fetches the LATEST version — which may differ from what a previously deployed workload uses. The claim 'same secrets as deployed workloads' is only true when a deployed stack exists with matching outputs.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to: 'In dev mode, $Secret() resolves the secret value from AWS Secrets Manager. If a deployed stack exists, it uses the same pinned version; otherwise it fetches the latest version.'

positiveFindings: The $Secret() resolution mechanism is accurately described — the source confirms it fetches the secret, extracts VersionId, and returns a CloudFormation dynamic reference: `{{resolve:secretsmanager:${secretName}:SecretString:${jsonKey || ''}::${secret.VersionId}}}`.
Version pinning behavior is correctly explained and directly supported by the VersionId inclusion in the dynamic reference string.
JSON dot-notation support is accurately described — the source splits on '.' and validates both JSON parsing and key existence with descriptive error messages.
Console page elements (AWS account selector, region selector, 'Create new secret' button, show/edit/delete icon buttons) are all confirmed by the SecretsPage.tsx source.
The comparison between $Secret() and $SsmParam() is factually grounded — both directives exist in built-in-directives.ts with the described backing stores and behaviors.
All cross-links in the draft point to valid routes in the navigation index (/cli/secret-create, /cli/secret-get, /cli/secret-delete, /configuration/directives, etc.).
The localResolveFunction for dev mode is correctly characterized as fetching the actual secret value rather than returning a dynamic reference.
The FAQ answer about 'Is there a secret:list CLI command? No.' is confirmed by the navigation index which lists only secret-create, secret-get, and secret-delete routes.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the provided `$Secret()` directive source, especially around dynamic references, version IDs, JSON key extraction, and Console list actions. The main publication risks are unsupported CLI behavior, unsupported auto-generation behavior, and a few claims that go beyond the two provided source files.

issues.statement: “For database resources (relational databases, Redis clusters), Stacktape detects missing password secrets during deployment and offers to generate them automatically… In non-interactive mode (CI/CD), auto-generation is skipped…”
issues.reason: The provided source for `$Secret()` only fetches an existing AWS Secrets Manager value and throws an error suggesting `secret:create` when the secret is missing. No provided source supports automatic generation for relational databases or Redis, URL-safe generated character sets, interactive generation prompts, or non-interactive skip behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the automatic secret generation section unless database/Redis deployment source is added. Based on the provided directive source, say that a missing secret makes `$Secret()` resolution fail and the CLI hint suggests creating it with `secret:create`.

evidence.quote: `If you did not create the secret yet, create it using ${tuiManager.prettyCommand('secret:create')}`

issues.statement: “If a secret with the same name already exists, the CLI prompts for confirmation before updating. Use `--forceUpdate` to skip the prompt in automated workflows.”
issues.reason: No CLI command source is provided for `secret:create`, so the prompt behavior and `--forceUpdate` flag are not grounded in the source list. The provided directive source only mentions a `secret:create` hint; it does not define flags or update behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the `--forceUpdate` claim and the update-by-recreate CLI example unless the `secret:create` command source is included. Keep only the sourced fact that missing secrets are hinted with `secret:create`.

evidence.quote: `If you did not create the secret yet, create it using ${tuiManager.prettyCommand('secret:create')}`

issues.statement: “`$Secret()` can be used anywhere a string value is accepted in your Stacktape config: environment variables, database credentials, resource properties, and more.”
issues.reason: The provided directive implementation returns a CloudFormation Secrets Manager dynamic reference during deployment. AWS only resolves dynamic references in supported CloudFormation resource properties; the source does not support the broader claim that every string position in Stacktape config can use `$Secret()`. This overstatement could make readers put `$Secret()` in unsupported string positions.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: “`$Secret()` returns a Secrets Manager dynamic reference during deployment, so use it in Stacktape config fields that are passed to CloudFormation properties that support dynamic references, such as workload environment variables and database password properties shown below.”

issues.statement: “The Secrets page in the Stacktape Console lets you create, view, edit, and delete secrets… When creating a secret in the Console, you provide Secret name, Region, Secret type, Secret value, and Description.”
issues.reason: The provided Console page source supports account and region selectors, a `Create new secret` button, and show/edit/delete actions. It does not include the create modal source, so the exact create form fields and secret type options are unsupported by the provided sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced Console behavior: the page lists secrets for an AWS account and region, supports sorting/pagination, and opens create/show/update/delete modals. Remove the exact create-form field list unless `CreateNewModal.tsx` is added to the source list.

issues.statement: “Secrets are region-scoped. A secret created in `eu-west-1` is only available to stacks deployed in `eu-west-1`.”
issues.reason: The Console source clearly scopes listing by selected AWS region, but the `$Secret()` directive source calls `awsSdkManager.getSecretValue({ secretId: secretName })` without a region argument. The provided source does not establish that `$Secret()` can only resolve secrets in the same deployment region, even though AWS Secrets Manager itself is regional.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to the sourced Console behavior: “The Console manages secrets for a selected AWS region.” If you want the stronger deployment-region claim, add AWS SDK manager or deployment region source.

issues.statement: “Stacktape provides two directives for sensitive values… `$SsmParam()` supports SecureString… Use `$SsmParam()` when you have existing SSM parameters or need to share non-secret configuration values across stacks.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports `$SsmParam()` and shows SecureString uses `ssm-secure`, but the recommendation that Stacktape provides exactly two directives for sensitive values and that `$SsmParam()` is intended for shared non-secret config is not grounded. The source lists many directives, and `$SsmParam()` can resolve both SecureString and non-secure SSM parameters.
issues.suggestedFix: Reword to: “`$Secret()` reads from AWS Secrets Manager. `$SsmParam()` reads from AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store and uses CloudFormation `ssm-secure` dynamic references for SecureString parameters.” Avoid claiming these are the only sensitive-value directives or prescribing non-secret usage unless sourced elsewhere.

issues.statement: The draft uses `RdsEnginePostgres` with `version: '18.1'` and `instanceSize: 'db.t3.micro'` in a Stacktape config example.
issues.reason: The provided source files do not include relational database type definitions or exported class names, so the verifier cannot confirm the `RdsEnginePostgres` class name, engine version value, or instance size property shape. This may be valid in the package, but it is not grounded in the supplied source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the relevant `types/stacktape-config` relational database source to the source list, or simplify the example to avoid unsourced database engine details and focus only on `$Secret('mainDatabase.password')`.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly describes `$Secret('secretName.keyName')` dot notation for extracting a JSON key; the directive splits the reference on `.` and parses `SecretString` as JSON when a key is present.
The draft correctly states that deployment-time `$Secret()` resolution records the current Secrets Manager version ID and returns a CloudFormation `{{resolve:secretsmanager:...}}` dynamic reference.
The draft correctly states that local/dev resolution fetches the secret value directly from AWS Secrets Manager using the stack output version ID when available.
The Console claims about sorting, pagination, selected AWS account, selected AWS region, and show/edit/delete icon actions are mostly grounded in `SecretsPage.tsx`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the central `$Secret()` workflow and its examples are mostly demonstrative, but it over-documents behavior that is not present in the provided source files. The biggest risks are unsupported Console form details, unsupported automatic secret generation behavior, and a comparison table that claims `$SsmParam()` feature behavior beyond the directive source.

issues.statement: “When creating a secret in the Console, you provide: Secret name, Region, Secret type, Secret value, Description.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source proves the Secrets page has AWS account and AWS region selectors plus a Create new secret button, but the modal implementation that would prove the create form fields is not included. issues.reason: None of the provided source files include database password generation behavior. The `$Secret()` directive source only fetches an existing secret and throws an error with a `secret:create` hint if fetching fails.

issues.statement: The `$Secret()` vs `$SsmParam()` table claims `$SsmParam()` does not support JSON keys, `$Secret()` supports SecureString, and `$Secret()` is auto-generated for databases.
issues.reason: The provided directive source supports `$Secret('secret.key')` JSON key extraction and `$SsmParam()` dynamic references, but it does not define a `$Secret()` SecureString concept or any database auto-generation behavior. `$SsmParam()` distinguishes `SecureString` only for SSM parameters.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the table with a narrower comparison grounded in the directive source: `$Secret()` reads from AWS Secrets Manager and can select one top-level JSON key with dot notation; `$SsmParam()` reads from SSM Parameter Store and uses `ssm-secure` only when the SSM parameter type is `SecureString`.

issues.statement: “Secrets never appear in your config files or version control.”
issues.reason: The source proves `$Secret()` returns a CloudFormation dynamic reference rather than the secret value, but it cannot prove users never place secrets in config files or version control. This is an absolute operational claim outside the implementation.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase this as intended usage: “Using `$Secret()` keeps the secret value out of `stacktape.ts`; commit only the secret name, not the secret value.”

issues.statement: The “JSON secrets with dot notation” section says `$Secret('secretName.keyName')` extracts a JSON key, but does not mention the source-supported limitation that parsing is based on `secretReference.split('.')`.
issues.reason: The source splits the secret reference on periods and destructures only the first two segments. That supports a simple `secret.key` shape, not clearly nested JSON paths such as `secret.database.password`. The draft’s “dot notation” wording can be read as general nested path support.
issues.suggestedFix: Say “Use `$Secret('secretName.keyName')` to read one top-level key from a JSON secret.” Avoid implying nested path traversal unless a source proves it.

issues.statement: “The `$Secret()` directive pins to a specific secret version at deploy time, so running workloads continue using the version that was current when they were last deployed.”
issues.reason: The source proves Stacktape records the secret VersionId and returns a Secrets Manager dynamic reference containing that VersionId. It does not prove the runtime behavior of already-running workloads after an update, especially across all resource types.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the deploy-time version claim, but narrow the runtime implication: “Stacktape records the current secret version ID during deployment and uses that version in the generated dynamic reference; redeploy after updating a secret so generated configuration references the new version.”

issues.statement: “Use `$Secret()` for most sensitive values. Use `$SsmParam()` when you have existing SSM parameters or need to share non-secret configuration values across stacks.”
issues.reason: The source shows both directives and their backing AWS lookup mechanisms, but it does not support the recommendation that SSM Parameter Store is for sharing non-secret configuration across stacks. That may be good guidance, but it is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Ground the comparison in available behavior: `$Secret()` resolves AWS Secrets Manager values; `$SsmParam()` resolves AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store values and uses secure dynamic references for SecureString parameters.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is 40-75 words, self-contained, and answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing — AI-extractable as written.

issues.statement: “If no stack has been deployed yet, it fetches the latest version.”
issues.reason: The local resolver passes `versionId: secretVersionId` to `getSecretValue`. If no deployed stack output exists, `secretVersionId` is undefined, and AWS SDK behavior may return the current version. However, the Stacktape source does not explicitly branch on “no stack deployed” or state “latest version,” so the draft turns an implementation side effect into a documented guarantee.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously: “In dev mode, Stacktape attempts to resolve the deployed stack’s pinned version ID. When no pinned version ID is available, the resolver calls AWS Secrets Manager without a version ID.”

issues.statement: “If you use dot notation on a secret that is not valid JSON, or reference a key that does not exist in the JSON object, the deployment fails with a descriptive error. The error names both the secret and the missing key.”
issues.reason: For invalid JSON, the error names the key and the secret. For a missing JSON key, the code checks `!parsedSecret[jsonKey]`, so falsey values such as an empty string, `0`, or `false` are treated as missing. The draft says only nonexistent keys fail, which is narrower than the implementation.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: “If dot notation is used, the secret must be a JSON object and the selected key must resolve to a truthy value; otherwise deployment fails with an error naming the secret and key.”

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is 40-75 words, self-contained, and answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing — AI-extractable as written.
